mappings.rationaleAI: Directly addresses the nature, scope, and methods of philosophy, the core subject of section A.
Section A.2 covers diverse views of the methods of philosophy.
Section B.1 covers metaphysics including its history, nature, scope, and core problems.
Section B.6 covers ethics including metaethics, normative ethics, and its relation to other disciplines.
Section B.7 covers political philosophy including its nature, scope, relation to political science, and history.
Section B.8 covers aesthetics including its nature, scope, development, problems, and relation to other disciplines.
Section B.2 covers the philosophy of nature and B.13 references the philosophy of science.
Section B.2.e specifically lists the philosophy of biology as part of the philosophy of nature.
Section B.2.d specifically lists the philosophy of physics as part of the philosophy of nature.
Section B.14 references the philosophy of religion as a division of philosophy.
Section B.15 references the philosophy of law as a division of philosophy.
Section B.4 covers the philosophy of mind including its history, nature, scope, and problems.
Section B.3 covers epistemology or theory of knowledge including its history, nature, scope, and problems.

mappings.vsiAuthor: Emma Borg and Sarah A. Fisher
mappings.rationaleAI: Section B.9 covers the philosophy of language, and meaning is its central problem.
Section B.10 references the philosophy of logic as a division of philosophy.
Consciousness is central to the philosophy of mind (B.4) and its relation to empirical sciences.
Free will is a core problem in both philosophy of mind (B.4.c) and ethics (B.6.c).
Section B.1.c covers problems in metaphysics including questions about the nature of reality.
Causation is a core problem in metaphysics (B.1.c) and the philosophy of nature (B.2.c).
Time is a core problem in metaphysics (B.1.c) and the philosophy of nature (B.2.c).
Utilitarianism is a major normative ethical theory relevant to the types of ethics in B.6.a.
Virtue ethics is a major ethical tradition relevant to the types and problems of ethics in B.6.
Environmental ethics relates to problems in ethics (B.6.c) and the relation of ethics to other knowledge (B.6.b).
Animal rights relates to problems in ethics (B.6.c) and philosophical anthropology (B.5).
Medical ethics relates to the relation of ethics to other branches of knowledge (B.6.b).
The concept of evil connects to problems in ethics (B.6.c) and metaphysics (B.1.c).
Love connects to problems in philosophy of mind (B.4.c), ethics (B.6.c), and philosophical anthropology (B.5.c).
Beauty is a central problem in aesthetics (B.8.c) and the aesthetic experience (B.8.b).
The meaning of life connects to philosophical anthropology (B.5.c), metaphysics (B.1.c), and ethics (B.6.c).
Conscience relates to problems in ethics (B.6.c) and philosophy of mind (B.4.c).
Toleration is a problem in political philosophy (B.7) and ethics (B.6).
Objectivity is a core epistemological problem (B.3.c) and relates to criteria of truth (A.4).
Analytic philosophy represents a major school with distinctive methods (A.2) and conceptions of philosophy (A.1).
Continental philosophy represents an alternative conception of philosophy (A.1) with distinct methods (A.2).
Hermeneutics represents a philosophical method (A.2) connecting to epistemology (B.3) and philosophy of language (B.9).
Feminist philosophy engages with ethics (B.6), political philosophy (B.7), and philosophical anthropology (B.5).
The existence and attributes of God connect to metaphysics (B.1), philosophy of religion (B.14), and natural theology.
Atheism engages with the philosophy of religion (B.14) and metaphysical questions about existence.
Agnosticism connects to epistemology (B.3) and philosophy of religion (B.14).
Humanism relates to philosophical anthropology (B.5), ethics (B.6), and philosophy of religion (B.14).
Secularism connects to political philosophy (B.7) and philosophy of religion (B.14).
Information relates to epistemology (B.3) and the philosophy of language (B.9).
Infinity connects to the philosophy of mathematics (B.11) and metaphysics (B.1.c).
The concept of nothing connects to metaphysical problems (B.1.c) about being, existence, and reality.
Imagination connects to aesthetics (B.8), philosophy of mind (B.4), and phenomenology.
Thought is central to philosophy of mind (B.4) and epistemology (B.3).
The demarcation of science from pseudoscience is a core problem in philosophy of science (B.13).
